talk_title: Agentic AI for accelerating biomedical discovery
MOA_summary: Bi Omni is an open‑source AI agent that integrates a curated environment of >100 biomedical tools, databases, and wet‑lab robots, enabling it to plan, execute, and iteratively refine experimental protocols, data analyses, and hypothesis generation, thereby automating and accelerating the scientific discovery loop.
key_findings: Bi Omni performed a full plasmid cloning design in ~10 minutes, producing a protocol that was experimentally validated as correct.
The agent completed a 30‑human multimodal data analysis (458 Excel files) in ~35 minutes, a task estimated to take weeks by a post‑doc.

In internal benchmarks across variant prioritization, GWAS gene discovery, perturbation screen design, scRNA‑seq analysis, drug repurposing, microbiome taxa analysis, and rare‑disease diagnosis, Bi Omni achieved accuracy comparable to or exceeding specialist tools despite no task‑specific engineering.
Over a three‑month public beta, >10,000 users executed ~70,000 automated research tasks, demonstrating broad utility across academia, pharma, and clinical settings.

talk_title: Foundation and virtual cell models for single‑cell biology

models.name: XCell Ultra
models.genotype_or_key_features: 5 billion parameters, trained on Pisces dataset (≈26 M perturbed scRNA‑seq cells, 16 contexts)

models.name: BioReason Pro
models.genotype_or_key_features: 4 billion‑parameter language model fine‑tuned on protein GO reasoning data

MOA_summary: XCell extends the first‑generation scGBT foundation model by training a diffusion‑based language model on massive, diverse perturbation scRNA‑seq datasets (Pisces) to learn causal gene‑expression responses, while BioReason Pro aligns DNA‑ or protein‑sequence foundation models with large‑language‑model reasoning to predict pathway or GO‑term functions, using reinforcement learning and multimodal tokenization.
readouts: benchmark accuracy on perturbation prediction tasks
key_findings: Training on 26 M perturbed cells across 16 biological contexts enabled XCell to recover known protein‑protein interaction networks and improve causal inference over descriptive models.
Scaling XCell from 50 M to 5 B parameters yielded a monotonic decrease in training loss and increase in test‑set accuracy, establishing a clear scaling law for virtual‑cell models.
BioReason Pro achieved >80 % of predictions judged superior to human expert annotations on 200 novel proteins, outperforming baseline methods across GO‑term categories.
Both XCell and BioReason Pro were released open‑source; within two weeks, tens of thousands of users accessed the models, indicating strong community uptake.
open_questions: How will virtual‑cell models generalize to patient‑level organoid or in‑vivo perturbation data with limited training examples?
Can DNA‑sequence reasoning models be extended to predict drug‑response phenotypes directly from genomic variants?

talk_title: Real‑time AI augmentation for sepsis and palliative‑care in the clinic

speaker.name: Suchi Saria
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University; Founder & CEO, Bayesian Health

models.name: Sepsis detection AI (Cleveland Clinic deployment)
models.genotype_or_key_features: mixture‑of‑experts transformer on multimodal EMR data

models.name: Palliative‑care early‑alert AI (Mayo Clinic deployment)
models.genotype_or_key_features: same architecture applied to oncology‑related admissions

clinical_data.value: significant decrease (p<0.01) in in‑hospital mortality for sepsis cohort
≈15% relative reduction in oncology palliative‑care cohort
clinical_data.population: oncology patients eligible for palliative care

MOA_summary: A real‑time multimodal transformer model ingests continuous EMR streams (labs, vitals, notes, meds) and outputs patient‑specific risk scores; integrated as an EHR‑embedded modular agent, it surfaces transparent alerts, auto‑generates order‑sets and documentation, and feeds back clinician responses for continuous monitoring and drift detection.
key_findings: AI‑augmented sepsis detection achieved 80%‑90% sensitivity while reducing false‑positive alerts by ~10‑fold compared with legacy rule‑based systems.
Median time‑to‑treatment was advanced by 3.5 hours, leading to a statistically significant reduction in in‑hospital mortality (p<0.01).
Clinician adoption of AI alerts reached 80%‑90% across four health systems, demonstrating high workflow integration.
In oncology‑focused palliative‑care deployment, early alerts increased appropriate consults and cut 30‑day readmissions by ~15%, also lowering total cost of care.
A governance layer continuously monitors model drift, enabling rapid root‑cause analysis and model re‑training without interrupting care.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Initial rule‑based alerts suffered from high nuisance‑alert rates (≈20:1 false‑positive to true‑positive), which the AI system reduced to ~3:1.

combination_strategies.rationale: Embed risk scores directly into clinician order‑sets to reduce friction and improve adoption
combination_strategies.phenotype: higher alert acknowledgment and faster treatment initiation

open_questions: How does model performance vary across demographic sub‑populations and rare disease cohorts?
What are the optimal strategies for continual learning while preserving patient safety?

talk_title: Foundation models for pathology and multimodal patient representation

speaker.name: Faisal Mahmood
speaker.affiliation: Harvard Medical School, Department of Pathology

models.name: Titan
models.genotype_or_key_features: whole‑slide representation model trained with iBOT, contrastive image‑text, 1‑B+ parameters

models.name: Threads
models.genotype_or_key_features: multimodal contrastive model aligning H&E with molecular (IHC, transcriptomics) data

models.name: Virtual Patient Representation Model
models.genotype_or_key_features: trained on 7.2 M patients, 18 modalities, dynamic embedding updated over time

MOA_summary: Self‑supervised foundation models (e.g., Titan) learn dense embeddings of whole‑slide pathology images; multimodal contrastive training (Threads) aligns these embeddings with molecular assays, while a large‑scale multimodal patient encoder integrates structured and unstructured clinical data into a single dynamic vector for downstream prediction of diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment response.
key_findings: Titan achieved state‑of‑the‑art performance on slide‑level classification tasks while using a single vector per slide, outperforming prior region‑based models.
Contrastive training with IHC and transcriptomic data (Threads) improved few‑shot classification accuracy by >20 % on external cancer cohorts.
The 7.2 M‑patient multimodal encoder clustered known disease ontologies without supervision, demonstrating emergent medical knowledge.
Similarity search using the patient embeddings retrieved clinically relevant matches for rare‑cancer cases and potential trial candidates.
open_questions: How will dynamic patient embeddings handle longitudinal data drift and evolving clinical practice?
Can the virtual patient model be prospectively validated for trial matching in real time?
